(born March 5, 1976) is an American retired [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") (MMA) fighter, [professional wrestler](/wiki/Professional_wrestler "Professional wrestler"), and a former two-time [UFC Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/UFC_Heavyweight_Championship "UFC Heavyweight Championship"). He has competed as a [Super Heavyweight](/wiki/Super_Heavyweight_(MMA) "Super Heavyweight (MMA)") and [Heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)"). While he is best known for competing in the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship"), Sylvia has also fought for [Affliction](/wiki/Affliction_Entertainment "Affliction Entertainment"), the International Fighting Championships (IFC), and [ONE FC](/wiki/ONE_FC "ONE FC").

**Timothy Deane Sylvia** (born March 5, 1976) is an American retired [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") (MMA) fighter, [professional wrestler](/wiki/Professional_wrestler "Professional wrestler"), and a former two-time [UFC Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/UFC_Heavyweight_Championship "UFC Heavyweight Championship"). He has competed as a [Super Heavyweight](/wiki/Super_Heavyweight_(MMA) "Super Heavyweight (MMA)") and [Heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)"). While he is best known for competing in the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship"), Sylvia has also fought for [Affliction](/wiki/Affliction_Entertainment "Affliction Entertainment"), the International Fighting Championships (IFC), and [ONE FC](/wiki/ONE_FC "ONE FC").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Sylvia was born and raised in [Ellsworth, Maine](/wiki/Ellsworth,_Maine "Ellsworth, Maine"), attending Ellsworth High School and graduating in 1992. He joined a [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate") school as a child, and began [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling") in high school. After graduation, he worked construction, [community gardening](/wiki/Community_gardening "Community gardening"), hanging sheet rock, as a bouncer, and painting houses. After high school, he played semi-pro [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") for three years before becoming interested in MMA, and began to train with [Marcus Davis](/wiki/Marcus_Davis "Marcus Davis"), a fellow bouncer.[[2]](#cite_note-2) During this time Sylvia decided to take up [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling"). After training for a year, and winning [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling") tournaments, he got the opportunity to fight in a Rhode Island [no holds barred](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") amateur event, in which he knocked out his opponent in 17 seconds.[[3]](#cite_note-bio-3)

A longtime UFC fan, Sylvia and some friends attended *[UFC 28](/wiki/UFC_28 "UFC 28")* in [Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"), in 2000. In late 2000 Sylvia sold all of his belongings and moved to [Bettendorf, Iowa](/wiki/Bettendorf,_Iowa "Bettendorf, Iowa"), to train with [Team Miletich](/wiki/Miletich_Fighting_Systems "Miletich Fighting Systems") which had members such as former [UFC champions](/wiki/List_of_UFC_champions "List of UFC champions") [Matt Hughes](/wiki/Matt_Hughes_(fighter) "Matt Hughes (fighter)") and [Jens Pulver](/wiki/Jens_Pulver "Jens Pulver").[[3]](#cite_note-bio-3) Sylvia was promoted to a purple belt in [Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu "Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu") on October 31, 2011.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

### Early professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early professional career")]

Sylvia made his professional MMA debut in 2001 fighting for the IFC. He went on to rack up thirteen consecutive wins with ten knockouts in organizations such as the Hawaii-based SuperBrawl, and Extreme Challenge.

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

In 2002, Sylvia signed with the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") and won a TKO victory over [Wesley "Cabbage" Correira](/wiki/Wesley_Correira "Wesley Correira") at *[UFC 39](/wiki/UFC_39 "UFC 39")* when Correira's corner threw in the towel. Sylvia then went on to defeat [Ricco Rodriguez](/wiki/Ricco_Rodriguez "Ricco Rodriguez") at *[UFC 41](/wiki/UFC_41 "UFC 41")*, winning the UFC heavyweight championship for the first time. Soon after Sylvia defended his title with another quick victory over [Gan McGee](/wiki/Gan_McGee "Gan McGee") at *[UFC 44](/wiki/UFC_44 "UFC 44")*.

#### Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Controversy")]

After the fight with McGee, Sylvia tested positive for the banned substance [Stanozolol](/wiki/Stanozolol "Stanozolol"). He voluntarily forfeited his title and was handed a six-month suspension, and fined $10,000 by the [Nevada State Athletic Commission](/wiki/Nevada_State_Athletic_Commission "Nevada State Athletic Commission") (NSAC). He later commented that the steroid usage was for shedding excess weight. Sylvia apologized and made no effort to claim innocence.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

#### Injury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Injury")]

At *[UFC 48](/wiki/UFC_48 "UFC 48")* in June 2004, Sylvia returned to face [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir") for the vacant [UFC Heavyweight Championship](/wiki/UFC_Heavyweight_Championship "UFC Heavyweight Championship"); he lost. Early on in the fight Mir trapped Sylvia's right arm in an [armbar](/wiki/Armbar "Armbar") submission attempt. As Sylvia tried to escape the hold, Sylvia's right [radius bone](/wiki/Radius_(bone) "Radius (bone)") snapped about three inches below his elbow. Referee [Herb Dean](/wiki/Herb_Dean "Herb Dean") immediately stopped the fight and declared Sylvia unable to continue. Sylvia took exception to the decision and repeatedly claimed his arm was not broken (though the break could be explicitly seen on the slow-motion replay of the fight), even touching it and moving it around to demonstrate. Sylvia was taken to a local hospital where an x-ray revealed that his arm was in fact broken; he then took several months off to recuperate. Afterward, Sylvia said he was glad the referee stopped the fight, thereby saving his arm from further damage.

#### Return

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Return")]

Six months after his loss to [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir"), Sylvia returned (a titanium plate had been attached to his forearm) to the UFC in February 2005, taking on [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski") to crown an interim UFC heavyweight champion as Mir, while still the nominal UFC champion, had suffered numerous injuries from a motorcycle accident that left him unable to defend his title. Sylvia was knocked to the ground by an overhand right punch, and while on the ground was caught in an Achilles lock and submitted.

In May 2005, Sylvia defeated Mike Block in the IFC by TKO, then went on to face MMA veteran [Tra Telligman](/wiki/Tra_Telligman "Tra Telligman") at *[UFC 54](/wiki/UFC_54 "UFC 54")*. With one second left in the first round, Sylvia landed a left head kick which [knocked out](/wiki/Knockout "Knockout") Telligman. Sylvia made his cable television debut next, fighting [Assuério Silva](/wiki/Assu%C3%A9rio_Silva "Assuério Silva") at *[Ultimate Fight Night 3](/wiki/Ultimate_Fight_Night_3 "Ultimate Fight Night 3")* on [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV"), winning a unanimous decision after three rounds in a match that was intended to decide the next number one contender.

#### Reclaiming the title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Reclaiming the title")]

At *[UFC 59](/wiki/UFC_59 "UFC 59")*, Sylvia was finally awarded his rematch with the then-outright champion [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski"). After being knocked down by Arlovski, Sylvia won the fight via knockout in round one.

Sylvia next defended his championship in a trilogy fight with Andrei Arlovski at [UFC 61](/wiki/UFC_61 "UFC 61"). Sylvia utilized his reach and size advantage to win the fight by unanimous decision.

Sylvia next fought Mundials world Jiu-Jitsu champion, 2-time ADCC world champion and Jiu-Jitsu Pan American champion [Jeff Monson](/wiki/Jeff_Monson "Jeff Monson") at *[UFC 65](/wiki/UFC_65 "UFC 65")*. Billed as a classic "striker vs. grappler" match, Sylvia won again via unanimous decision by keeping the fight on the outside with the jab.

On March 3, 2007, Sylvia fought former UFC Heavyweight and UFC Light Heavyweight champion [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture") at [UFC 68](/wiki/UFC_68 "UFC 68"). After being knocked down by Couture he lost the fight and title by decision.

On October 20, 2007, at [UFC 77](/wiki/UFC_77 "UFC 77"), Sylvia defeated [Brandon Vera](/wiki/Brandon_Vera "Brandon Vera") via unanimous decision (29–28, 29–27, 29–28). In the post-fight interview he called out [Cheick Kongo](/wiki/Cheick_Kongo "Cheick Kongo") to fight for the spot as number one contender for the heavyweight championship belt.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On February 2, 2008, Sylvia lost via guillotine choke to [Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rodrigo_Nogueira "Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira") at [UFC 81](/wiki/UFC_81 "UFC 81") in Las Vegas for the interim heavyweight championship. This fight earned him a $65,000 *[Fight of the Night](/wiki/List_of_UFC_bonus_award_recipients "List of UFC bonus award recipients")* award.[[7]](#cite_note-UFC81-7)

Early in 2008, Sylvia requested and was granted a release from his UFC contract, which still had one fight remaining on it. Sylvia cited unhappiness with pay, desire to face [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko"), the non-exclusive contracts of other organizations, and the opportunity to fight more often as his reasons for leaving.[[8]](#cite_note-sylviagone-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Post-UFC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-UFC")]

On March 28, 2008, Sylvia signed with [Adrenaline MMA](/wiki/Adrenaline_MMA "Adrenaline MMA"), formerly [M-1 Global](/wiki/M-1_Global "M-1 Global"), to a two-year contract that allowed him to fight for other organizations while under contract with Adrenaline. A few weeks later he was offered to fight [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko") at [Affliction Clothing](/wiki/Affliction_Clothing "Affliction Clothing")'s inaugural event [Affliction: Banned](/wiki/Affliction:_Banned "Affliction: Banned"). The fight took place on July 19, 2008, and saw the Russian dropping Sylvia and taking his back before securing a rear naked choke, forcing Sylvia to submit at 0:36 of the first round.

On February 25, 2009, Sylvia's manager (and [Adrenaline MMA](/wiki/Adrenaline_MMA "Adrenaline MMA") [CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO")) Monte Cox announced that Sylvia would face former boxing world heavyweight champion and [1988 Olympic](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics") gold medalist [Ray Mercer](/wiki/Ray_Mercer "Ray Mercer") in a boxing main event at *Adrenaline MMA III* on May 30, 2009, at the [Trump Taj Mahal Casino Resort](/wiki/Trump_Taj_Mahal_Casino_Resort "Trump Taj Mahal Casino Resort") in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City "Atlantic City"). However, after the New Jersey State Athletic Control Board refused to sanction the fight, the event was moved to Birmingham Jefferson Civic Center in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), and rescheduled to June 13. Only days before the bout, it was changed to be contested under MMA rules, again because of sanctioning problems. When they finally met, Mercer knocked Sylvia out with a single punch nine seconds into the first round.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Sylvia weighed in at 310.6 pounds for the Super Heavyweight fight.

Before his bout with Mercer, Sylvia was scheduled to face [Paul Buentello](/wiki/Paul_Buentello "Paul Buentello") at *[Affliction: Trilogy](/wiki/Affliction:_Trilogy "Affliction: Trilogy").* But after the devastating KO loss, Cox and Affliction decided it would be best for Sylvia to sit out the next few months, because of his recent losses.

After Affliction folded, Sylvia defeated Jason Riley via TKO (punches) 2:32 into the first round at the *Adrenaline IV* [main event](/wiki/Main_event "Main event") on September 18, 2009, in Council Bluffs, Iowa.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was later revealed through Sherdog.com that Sylvia broke his right hand about a minute and a half into his fight with Riley, spoiling a debut with the Japanese promotion [Dream](/wiki/DREAM_(mixed_martial_arts) "DREAM (mixed martial arts)"), planned for October 25.

Sylvia's next fight was expected to be a rematch with [The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter") alumni [Wes Sims](/wiki/Wes_Sims_(fighter) "Wes Sims (fighter)") on March, 20th in Ohio. However, the Ohio State Athletic Commission refused to sanction the bout, saying that the fight was "non-competitive",[[13]](#cite_note-13) resulting in the bout being rescheduled to June 6, 2010, in Nova Scotia, Canada, for the IFC Superheavyweight title.

In the meantime, Sylvia fought five time World's Strongest Man [Mariusz Pudzianowski](/wiki/Mariusz_Pudzianowski "Mariusz Pudzianowski") at Moosin: God of Martial Arts on May 21, 2010, winning by submission (punches).

The Sims fight was scuttled again (as well as another against UFC veteran [Pedro Rizzo](/wiki/Pedro_Rizzo "Pedro Rizzo")), when it was discovered that Sylvia broke his foot at Moosin.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Sylvia defeated [Paul Buentello](/wiki/Paul_Buentello "Paul Buentello") by Knockout due to an uppercut at Powerhouse World Promotions: War on the Mainland on August 14.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
As heavyweight fighter [Pedro Rizzo](/wiki/Pedro_Rizzo "Pedro Rizzo") entered the ring during the post fight interview, Powerhouse World Promotions announced Sylvia would make his first title defense against the fellow UFC veteran. The fight never occurred, however, as PWP disappeared after this inaugural event.

Sylvia fought Vince Lucero at CFX/Extreme Challenge on December 11.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Sylvia won the fight by submission due to punches.

### Titan Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Titan Fighting Championship")]

Sylvia fought Abe Wagner at Titan Fighting Championships 16 (Sylvia replaced [Todd Duffee](/wiki/Todd_Duffee "Todd Duffee")), Sylvia weighed in at 311 pounds and was defeated 32 seconds into the first round, ending his four-fight win streak.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Fight Tour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fight Tour")]

Sylvia was originally scheduled to face Shayne Adams (1–2) on August 13 at a Fight Tour event at the Rockford Metro Centre in Rockford, Illinois.[[19]](#cite_note-19) But due to a scheduling conflict with the athletic commission the event will now take place on August 20.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Sylvia was then scheduled to face Brian Heden at the August 20 Fight Tour event because the Illinois Athletic Commission would not approve the fight between Adams and Sylvia.[[21]](#cite_note-21) However the bout between Sylvia and Heden was later scrapped for unknown reasons and Sylvia would now face submission specialist Patrick Barrentine.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Sylvia defeated Barrentine via TKO in the first round.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Sylvia weighed in at 280.5 pounds for the bout, 30 pounds lighter than his previous bout against Wagner.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### ProElite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: ProElite")]

Sylvia was expected to take on [Pedro Rizzo](/wiki/Pedro_Rizzo "Pedro Rizzo") at ProElite 2 on November 5,[[25]](#cite_note-25) However Rizzo later pulled out of the bout due to injury.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
Sylvia instead competed against [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") heavyweight Andreas Kraniotakes[[27]](#cite_note-27) and won the fight via unanimous decision.

### New England Fights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: New England Fights")]

Sylvia fought against Randy Smith at NEF: Fight Night 3 on June 16 in his home state of Maine.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
Sylvia defeated Smith in 12 seconds via TKO.

### ONE Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: ONE Fighting Championship")]

In mid-July 2012, it was announced that Tim Sylvia had signed with the Asia-based promotion [ONE Championship](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) The promotion quickly announced that Sylvia would face [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski") in his debut, marking the fourth time the two fighters had faced each other.
Sylvia and Arlovski faced off at [ONE Fighting Championship: Pride of a Nation](/wiki/ONE_Fighting_Championship:_Pride_of_a_Nation "ONE Fighting Championship: Pride of a Nation") on August 31, 2012, in [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"). The fight between Tim Sylvia and Andrei Arlovski ended in a no-contest due to an illegal soccer kick by Andrei Arlovski. According to ONE FC rules at the time, soccer kicks are legal only if the referee clears the fighter to do so after determining the grounded fighter can still intelligently defend himself. Arlovski received no such clearance, the kick was deemed illegal. Four days later, One FC eliminated the clearance restriction.

On May 31, 2013, Sylvia fought [Tony Johnson](/wiki/Tony_Johnson_(fighter) "Tony Johnson (fighter)") at [ONE FC 9: Rise to Power](/wiki/ONE_FC_Events#ONE_Fighting_Championship:_Rise_to_Power "ONE FC Events").[[31]](#cite_note-31) Sylvia lost by TKO (doctor stoppage) in 3:25 of the third round.

### Inoki Genome Federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Inoki Genome Federation")]

Sylvia fought [Satoshi Ishii](/wiki/Satoshi_Ishii "Satoshi Ishii") at [Inoki Bom-Ba-Ye](/wiki/Inoki_Bom-Ba-Ye "Inoki Bom-Ba-Ye") and lost by unanimous decision.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Fight Nights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Fight Nights")]

Sylvia fought Dagestan prospect [Ruslan Magomedov](/wiki/Ruslan_Magomedov "Ruslan Magomedov") at Fight Nights: Battle of Moscow 13 and lost by unanimous decision.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Retirement")]

Sylvia was scheduled to face Juliano Coutinho at Reality Fighting on January 3, 2015, however, he was denied medical clearance by the Mohegan Athletic Unit, and as a result the fight was cancelled. Sylvia weighed in at 371 pounds for the fight. When asked why the fight was cancelled Monte Cox, Sylvia's manager, wrote on Facebook “Not 100 [percent] sure... because of his age he had to do a lot of meds at last minute,” Cox wrote. “In all he needed a stress test, MRI, EKG, eye, blood and physical." Apparently all the tests ordered by the Mohegan commission have made a significant impact on Sylvia's bank account, as he wrote on Cox's Facebook thread that he is “[$2,000] in the hole because of all the [tests] I had to do.[[34]](#cite_note-34) After the cancelled fight Sylvia announced his retirement from MMA. He appeared in the cage alongside his proposed opponent and said an MRI issue prevented him from competing. “This is the end of my career,” Sylvia said at Mohegan Sun Arena, adding, “They said that I’ve received enough damage over 16 years.”[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Professional wrestling career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Professional wrestling career")]

On September 25, 2010, Sylvia made his [pro wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") debut, losing via submission to fellow mixed-martial-artist [Josh Barnett](/wiki/Josh_Barnett "Josh Barnett"), at [IGF](/wiki/Inoki_Genome_Federation "Inoki Genome Federation")'s Genome 13 event.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

A grassroots Twitter campaign started on August 1, 2013, for [Total Nonstop Action Wrestling](/wiki/Total_Nonstop_Action_Wrestling "Total Nonstop Action Wrestling") president [Dixie Carter](/wiki/Dixie_Carter_(wrestling) "Dixie Carter (wrestling)") to sign Sylvia and add him to the TNA ranks. Sylvia himself endorsed the campaign by re-tweeting it to his Twitter followers.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Slapfighting career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Slapfighting career")]

At the age of 47, Sylvia made his return to combat sports, competing at SlapFIGHT Championship 25 on April 22, 2023. Sylvia knocked out his opponent in the fifth round.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Sylvia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Sylvia has a son.[[40]](#cite_note-ufcwatn-40) In 2010, Sylvia confirmed that he is a police officer in the State of Illinois on a part-time basis (one day a month).[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)
